feedback.text: I am deeply appreciateive of this initiative as digital products marketing practices have become quite frankly predatory and an absolute chore to navigate around. Everything feels lke a scam and reading the fine prinpt has become a must for the most basic of purchases, when said fine prinpt isn't itself burried in an obscure corner. One area where I find this kins of deceptive practices particularly prevalent is video games. They've become a growing part of culture and will keep gaining shares over time, but marketing-wise it looks like the far-west. What bothers me is games that you "buy", but you actually buy a time-limited license that can be revoked at any time for any reason, based on terms of serive or end user license agreements that are selectively enforced. Games that you "buy", but are actually live service games that require a monthly subscription (without clearly stating it). Games that are "sold" before their official release (pre-ordering), promising features that are then never implemented (false advertising?). Loot boxes and other gambling-related microtransactions. "Pay to win" games where giving more money to the developer gives players a material advantage over other players, this should clearly be announced when "purchasing" the product. Not specific to video games but some platforms will also, apparently, selectively enforce their own license agreements or have different agreements for different people, which is flat out unfair / unjust (Twitch?). All these practices are shady at best, sometimes quite unethical, and I, as a consummer, have great difficulty dentifying and avoiding the compagnies that engage in such practices. What I would love is a requirement for digital products to disclose their key conditions in no uncertain terms. It could based on a template much like the Energy label on house appliances, so that I can identify at a glance if a product is for me or not. Key details should include information about: - one-time purchase (buying) vs recurring fees (subscribing). - license "purchase" or copy purchase. - commitment duration (monthly / yearly) - any kind of cancellation fee for subscriptions - TOS and EULA enforcement: arbitrary or not. Same for all or not. I understand that EULAs and TOS can be long because of all the legal jargon required. However. The key points highlighted above should not be hard to find. They should figure at the very top and like I said, be clearly defined in no uncertain terms. I want an energy sticker for digital products.
